data.title: The Tactical Trio Blueprint For AI Healthcare Stocks One Is An Imaging Pivot One Is A High Margin Compounder One Is A Quiet Cash Flow Engine
data.description:
Artificial intelligence in healthcare sits at the intersection of powerful software and real human need, at a time when energy shocks, uneven inflation and shifting bond markets keep wider assets on edge. While global growth expectations are softer and policy signals are mixed, AI tools that can help clinicians detect disease, personalise treatment and run hospitals more efficiently still speak to long term structural demand. This Transformative AI Healthcare screener is built to surface companies linked to those trends. In this article, you will see 3 stocks from the screener that stand out for further research.

Olympus (TSE:7733)

Overview: Olympus is a long established Japanese medtech company that builds endoscopy and surgical imaging systems used in hospitals worldwide, from gastrointestinal and respiratory procedures to urology, gynecology and ear, nose and throat surgery, alongside related devices, software and servicing.

Operations: Olympus generates the bulk of its revenue from Gastrointestinal Solutions at ¥630,099m and Surgical & Interventional Solutions at ¥357,254m, with sales spread across North America, Europe, Japan, China and the rest of Asia and Oceania.

Market Cap: ¥1.78t

For investors who want exposure to AI in real world clinical workflows rather than pure software plays, Olympus may warrant a closer look. The group is reshaping itself around two core divisions and rolling out platforms such as the VISERA ELITE III surgical imaging system, along with cloud based and AI assisted endoscopy tools. Analysts have noted expectations for earnings growth that outpaces the broader Japan market, together with a 1.86% dividend. At the same time, recent earnings guidance cuts, execution risk in China and elevated R&D spending keep pressure on management to deliver. For investors interested in the mix of AI adoption, global medtech scale and active capital allocation decisions, there is more to explore in the full story.

Olympus is reshaping around AI powered endoscopy and surgical imaging, yet recent guidance cuts and higher R&D spend raise fresh questions. Get the analysis report for Olympus to see what might be hiding behind the headline story.

eWeLLLtd (TSE:5038)

Overview: eWeLLLtd runs a suite of cloud tools that help Japan’s home based nursing providers handle everyday work, from electronic medical records and insurance claims to staff attendance, admin support, AI assisted visit reports, factoring of receivables and e learning for legal training.

Market Cap: ¥32.27b

eWeLLLtd operates at the intersection of healthcare, software and AI, with its iBow platform helping visiting nurses reduce paperwork and capture richer clinical data through AI powered reporting. Earnings and revenue forecasts in the available data point to growth that outpaces the wider Japanese market, supported by a 32.2% ROE and net margins above 30%, which together indicate that the core business is currently highly profitable. At the same time, a premium P/E multiple, full reliance on higher risk external borrowing and only 25% board independence introduce governance and funding questions that careful investors may want to weigh. For those tracking how AI is reshaping home care and what that could mean for long term returns, the finer details may matter more than the headline growth rates.

High margins and 32.2% ROE suggest eWeLLLtd’s home nursing platform might be doing more than the current P/E is letting on. At the same time, the reliance on external borrowing and governance mix hint at a twist in the analysis report for eWeLLLtd

FINDEX (TSE:3649)

Overview: FINDEX is a Tokyo based healthtech company that builds software and digital tools for hospitals and clinics, from image filing, electronic medical records and document automation to remote information sharing, AI generated medical text and workflow robots that cut routine admin work.

Operations: FINDEX generates most of its revenue from the Medical Business at ¥5,691m, with smaller contributions from the Public Sector Business at ¥355m and Health Tech Business at ¥63m, almost entirely in Japan where revenue totals about ¥6,109m.

Market Cap: ¥20.0b

FINDEX brings AI directly into day to day clinical work through tools like CocktailAI for medical text generation and DigiWorker for hospital automation, all on top of a long running medical records and imaging platform that currently supports net margins of 20.6%. Forecast earnings growth of 13.24% a year and historically high ROE above 23% are paired with a P/E that sits below many healthcare software peers. At the same time, full reliance on higher risk external borrowing and limited board independence mean the funding model and governance deserve closer attention as this healthtech stack expands across Japanese hospitals.

data.title: Vivenio Residencial SOCIMI (BME:YVIV) Revenue Trend Reinforces Bullish Growth Narratives
data.description:
Vivenio Residencial SOCIMI (BME:YVIV) has put fresh numbers on the table for FY 2025, reporting first half revenue of €37.7 million and basic EPS of €0.064. The trailing twelve months show revenue of €69.5 million and EPS of €0.121 on a share price of €1.35. The company has reported revenue of €28.1 million and EPS of €0.053 in the first half of 2024, followed by €31.8 million in revenue and EPS of €0.057 in the second half. This sets up FY 2025 with a results profile where investors may weigh higher earnings per share against a softer margin picture overall.

See our full analysis for Vivenio Residencial SOCIMI.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to see how these results line up against the widely held narratives about Vivenio Residencial SOCIMI and where the numbers start to challenge those views.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM net income of €87.8m shaped by €86.1m one off

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, net income excluding extra items is €87.8m alongside a disclosed one off gain of €86.1m that had a major impact on reported results.
  • Bears focus on earnings quality here, and the figures support that caution as well as set some limits on it:
    • Critics highlight that an €86.1m one off is almost the size of the €87.8m trailing net income figure, so it meaningfully distorts how "normal" profitability looks.
    • At the same time, five year earnings growth of 21.2% a year and a 23.2% rate over the last year show that longer term growth is not solely tied to that single gain.

Revenue and EPS step up each half year

  • Revenue moves from €28.1m in H1 2024 to €31.8m in H2 2024 and then €37.7m in H1 2025, with basic EPS reported at €0.053, €0.057 and €0.064 across those same periods.
  • A more optimistic angle points to this pattern of higher revenue and EPS, and the multi year growth rates help explain why:
    • Supporters point to the 21.2% yearly earnings growth over five years and the 23.2% rate in the most recent year as consistent with the step up in EPS from €0.053 to €0.064 across recent halves.
    • However, net profit margins are described as lower than a year earlier, which reminds readers that higher revenue and EPS do not automatically translate into stronger profitability per euro of sales.
To see how other investors connect these revenue and EPS trends to their long term stories for the stock, check out the 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Vivenio Residencial SOCIMI.

11.3x P/E versus DCF fair value of €0.14

  • The trailing P/E sits at 11.3x, below the 24.5x Global Residential REITs average, the 57.1x peer average and the 17.3x Spanish market, while a DCF fair value of €0.14 is given against the current share price of €1.35.
  • Skeptical views lean on this mix of valuation signals and balance sheet risk, and the data provides several concrete reference points:
    • The lower 11.3x P/E can be read as a discount compared with sector and market averages, yet it is being measured on earnings that include the large one off gain and lower current net margins.
    • Interest payments are described as not well covered by earnings, and together with the DCF fair value of €0.14 sitting below the €1.35 share price, this feeds into concerns about how much support trailing earnings really give to the current valuation.

Approach 1: Steven Madden Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes the cash Steven Madden is expected to generate in the future and discounts those amounts back into today’s dollars to estimate what the business could be worth now.

For Steven Madden, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $129.4 million. Analysts and Simply Wall St projections point to Free Cash Flow reaching about $519.1 million in 2035, with interim estimates such as $259 million in 2027 and $312 million in 2028. Amounts beyond the formal analyst horizon are extrapolated using Simply Wall St’s 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model.

Bringing all those projected cash flows back to today using a discount rate produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $80.54 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$38.38, the model suggests the stock trades at roughly a 52.3% discount, which points to a meaningful valuation gap on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Steven Madden Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Steven Madden, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. The higher the growth expectations and the lower the perceived risk, the higher a “normal” or “fair” P/E investors are usually willing to pay, and the reverse is also true.

Steven Madden currently trades on a P/E of 62.77x. That sits well above the Luxury industry average P/E of 20.14x and also above the peer average of 22.74x. On simple comparisons, the stock carries a much richer earnings multiple than many similar companies.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Steven Madden is 35.52x. Comparing the current P/E of 62.77x with the Fair Ratio of 35.52x suggests the shares trade at a premium to what this framework would point to.

data.description:
Progressive (PGR) opened 2026 with solid momentum, coming off Q4 2025 revenue of about US$22.7b and basic EPS of US$5.03, backed by trailing 12 month figures of US$87.6b in revenue and EPS of US$19.29, alongside year over year earnings growth of 33.6%.   • Over the last 12 months, Progressive reported a combined ratio of 87.4% alongside a net margin of 12.9%, compared with 11.2% the prior year, which means underwriting and overall profitability have both been running at relatively healthy levels for a major insurer.
  • What bullish investors highlight is that this combination of a sub 90% combined ratio and 33.6% earnings growth is exactly what they point to when arguing Progressive’s pricing and telematics capabilities can support strong profitability. The bearish narrative, however, counters that climate and cost inflation risks could still pressure that 87.4% combined ratio if claims volatility rises from here.
    • The bullish side leans on Progressive’s use of data and rapid rate adjustments to argue that underwriting margins can stay attractive even when costs move, which lines up with the improved net margin implied by the latest 12.9% figure.
    • Skeptics focus on the risk section that flags rising repair and healthcare costs plus weather events as threats to loss ratios, using those points to argue that today’s 87.4% combined ratio might sit above where they would like to see it in a more stressed claims environment.

A DCF model takes projected future cash flows and then discounts them back to today to estimate what the entire business might be worth in current dollars.

SAP, trading at €146.54, is moving to tighten the link between its core ERP platforms and global payment flows at a time when many enterprises are looking to simplify cross border commerce. The stock shows a mixed performance profile, with a 31.3% return over 3 years and 36.3% over 5 years, set against a 27.4% decline year to date and a 36.4% decline over 1 year. For investors watching XTRA:SAP, this kind of product move is directly aligned with SAP’s core strength of supporting complex global operations.

For enterprise clients already standardised on SAP, a certified, direct connection to BlueSnap could help reduce reliance on custom payment builds and multiple intermediaries. Looking ahead, the key things to watch are customer adoption of the integration, any visible impact on payment efficiency metrics, and how SAP positions similar partnerships across other financial workflows.

This BlueSnap integration sits squarely in SAP’s push to make its cloud ERP offerings more of a one stop operating hub for global enterprises. By plugging a payment provider directly into SAP Cloud ERP and S/4HANA, customers can run payment processing, reconciliation, fraud checks, and tokenized transactions without layering separate connectors on top. For companies with multi entity, multi currency structures, that kind of simplification can be attractive when comparing SAP against suites from Oracle or Microsoft, or against more modular setups that stitch together several vendors.

  • The PCI compliant, tokenization ready setup with fraud protection and multi entity support could appeal to complex global customers that value a single account structure for payments.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on how often SAP or BlueSnap reference customer wins that explicitly use this direct to core integration, any commentary on improved authorization rates or lower cross border costs, and whether SAP repeats this pattern across other financial workflows. Approach 1: Hasbro Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected future cash flows, then discounts them back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what a stream of cash flows could be worth now, given the time value of money and risk.

For Hasbro, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $687.2 million. Growth expectations and risk both influence what feels like a normal or fair P/S multiple. Higher expected growth or more resilient revenue can justify a higher multiple, while higher risk or weaker margins can point to a lower one.

  • Points to brand, product quality and management issues, along with heavier debt and ongoing dividends, as signs that the business model is under pressure and past decisions like the Entertainment One deal still weigh on the company.
  • Suggests that efforts to shift toward video gaming and digital projects have not translated into reliable cash flows, and that large parts of the portfolio could struggle to earn an adequate return without major change.

Taken together, these two Narratives frame the current share price as sitting between an analyst led fair value that is modestly above market and a very pessimistic view that applies a much lower value to the equity.
